title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita, 2024
Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between per capita energy from fossil fuels, measured in kilowatt-hours on the horizontal axis, and per capita CO₂ emissions, measured in tonnes on the vertical axis. Both axes utilize a logarithmic scale to accommodate the wide range of values across nations. The data points are color-coded by income group, distinguishing lower-middle-income countries in pink, upper-middle-income countries in light green, and high-income countries in dark green. Circle size corresponds to population, with a reference scale indicating that the largest circles represent populations of 1.4 billion. A strong positive correlation is visible, where higher fossil fuel energy consumption coincides with higher carbon emissions. Pakistan and the Philippines appear in the lower-left corner, showing the lowest values for both metrics, with energy consumption below 5,000 kWh and emissions under 1 tonne. Moving toward the center, India and Indonesia occupy the lower-middle range, while China and the United States sit in the upper-middle section with energy use between 20,000 and 50,000 kWh and emissions between 5 and 15 tonnes. At the extreme upper-right end, Qatar, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia display the highest intensity, with energy consumption exceeding 100,000 kWh and emissions surpassing 20 tonnes per person. The metric is age-standardized to allow comparisons between countries and over time, and not all deaths in a country may have been registered with a cause of death. The distribution shows a consistent upward trend where increased fossil fuel energy usage aligns with increased per capita carbon output.
